Press enter or click to view image in full size

Building a full-stack application often means managing multiple projects, separate dependencies, and duplicate configuration for the frontend and backend. Nx simplifies this by bringing everything into a single workspace. In this tutorial, you’ll build an Employee Management application using Angular, NestJS, GraphQL, Apollo Client, TypeORM, and SQL Server.
💡 Tip: The complete package.json is available at the end of this article if you want to compare your setup or copy it directly.
What We’ll Build
By the end of this tutorial you will build:

• Employee CRUD application

• Angular frontend

• NestJS backend

• GraphQL API

• SQL Server database

• Apollo Client integration
Along the way, I’ll explain every important concept in simple terms so even if you’re completely new to this stack, you’ll understand not only HOW to build it, but WHY each technology is used.
🏗️ Project Architecture & Data Flow
Before writing any code, let’s understand how data flows through our application. Imagine you click the View Profile button. The request doesn’t go directly to SQL Server. Instead, it passes through several layers, each responsible for one specific job.
Browser ➔ Angular UI ➔ Apollo Client ➔ GraphQL API ➔ Resolver ➔ Service ➔ TypeORM ➔ SQL Server========================================================================================
📥 THE REQUEST FLOW (DOWN)
========================================================================================
[1. Browser] --> User clicks "View Profile" (ID: 42)
│
▼
[2. Angular UI] --> Triggers event handler to pull data
│
▼
[3. Apollo Client] --> Generates GraphQL Request:
"query { getEmployee(id: 42) { name email } }"
│
▼ [ NETWORK BOUNDARY: HTTP POST ]
│
[4. GraphQL API] --> Validates incoming schema payload shape
│
▼
[5. NestJS Res.] --> Extract parameters: id = 42
│
▼
[6. NestJS Serv.] --> Runs business rules & initiates DB lookups
│
▼
[7. TypeORM] --> Generates native SQL command:
"SELECT name, email FROM employees WHERE id = 42;"
│
▼
[8. SQL Server] --> Scans physical storage disks for matching index rows
========================================================================================
↩️ THE RESPONSE FLOW (UP)
========================================================================================
[1. SQL Server] --> Returns Raw Row Data:
[id: 42, name: "Alice", email: "alice@co.com", salary: 95000]
│
▼
[2. TypeORM] --> Maps row array to a clean JavaScript Entity Object
│
▼
[3. NestJS Serv.] --> Post-processes values or runs logging
│
▼
[4. NestJS Res.] --> Filters out "salary" since client didn't request it
│
▼ [ NETWORK BOUNDARY: JSON RESPONSE ]
│
[5. Apollo Client] --> Receives filtered JSON: { data: { name: "Alice", email: "..." } }
Updates local client state cache
│
▼
[6. Angular UI] --> Explicitly binds the fresh data directly into the DOM
│
▼
[7. Browser] --> Re-renders text elements on screen for the user
========================================================================================
Without Nx vs. With Nx
===================================================================
❌ TRADITIONAL REPO (Without Nx) │ │ ✅ MONOREPO WORKSPACE (With Nx)
===================================================================
│ │
📁 root-project/ │ │ 📁 nx-monorepo-workspace/
├── 📁 frontend/ │ │ ├── 📁 apps/
│ ├── 📄 package.json │ │ │ ├── 📁 frontend/ (Angular)
│ └── 📁 node_modules/ │ │ │ └── 📁 backend/ (NestJS)
│ │ │ │
├── 📁 backend/ │ │ ├── 📁 libs/ (Shared Workspace Packages)
│ ├── 📄 package.json │ │ │ ├── 📁 data-models/
│ └── 📁 node_modules/ │ │ │ └── 📁 auth-validation/
│ │ │ │
└── ❌ Separate configurations │ │ ├── 📄 package.json (Single Root File)
❌ Redundant node_modules │ │ ├── 📁 node_modules/ (Unified Storage)
❌ Manual, isolated tooling │ │ └── ✅ Shared libraries & global tooling
│ │
===================================================================Nx doesn’t replace Angular or NestJS. It manages multiple applications in a single workspace, making it easy to share code, tools, and configuration while keeping each app independent for building and deployment.
🛠️ Tech Stack Demystified
Before diving into the terminal, let’s look at the pieces of our puzzle and what each one does:

• Nx: Manages both frontend and backend in one workspace.

• Angular: Builds the user interface.

• NestJS: Builds the backend API.

• GraphQL: Lets the frontend request exactly the data it needs.

• Apollo Client: Sends GraphQL requests from Angular.

• TypeORM: Communicates with SQL Server without writing raw SQL for every operation.

• SQL Server: Stores the application data securely.
Backend Technologies Deep-Dive

• Nx Monorepo: The “wrapper” that manages both our frontend and backend together in one repository.

• NestJS: A progressive Node.js framework for our backend API. It looks a lot like Angular, keeping our backend organized using Modules, Services, and Controllers.

• @nestjs/graphql & @nestjs/apollo: Integrates GraphQL with NestJS and automatically creates a GraphQL server from our decorators and resolvers.

• @apollo/server & graphql: Provides the GraphQL execution engine that processes queries and mutations.

• typeorm & @nestjs/typeorm: The database translator (ORM). Instead of writing raw SQL strings (like SELECT * FROM Employee), TypeORM maps database tables to TypeScript classes so we can interact with rows using clean methods like this.repository.find().

• class-validator & class-transformer: Validate and transform incoming request data before it reaches the application logic.
Frontend Technologies Deep-Dive

• Angular: Our frontend framework, utilizing modern Standalone Components and Angular Material for a sleek UI layout.

• @apollo/client: Apollo Client manages GraphQL requests and caches responses to improve performance. It automatically caches (remembers) data we have already fetched so our application feels faster when navigating between pages.

• apollo-angular: Integrates GraphQL with NestJS for the frontend. It wraps GraphQL data operations into native Angular RxJS Observables, allowing data to stream seamlessly directly into our UI layouts.

• graphql-tag (gql): The highlighter. It translates raw text blocks inside our TypeScript files so code compilers recognize them as active GraphQL syntax.
Part 1: Setting Up the Workspace
Open your terminal. We will initialize our workspace, install our tools, and let the CLI generate our folder structures automatically.
Step 1: Initialize the Nx Workspace
npx create-nx-workspace@latest employee-workspace --preset=apps --nxCloud=skip
cd employee-workspaceStep 2: Install Nx Framework Plugins
npm install -D @nx/angular @nx/nestThis command installs the Nx plugins for Angular and NestJS, allowing you to generate components, services, modules, and build your apps using Nx commands (e.g., npx nx g @nx/angular:component header or npx nx g @nx/nest:service users).
Step 3: Scaffold the Applications
# Generate the Angular Frontend
npx nx g @nx/angular:app apps/frontend --routing --style=scss --standalone# Generate the NestJS Backend
npx nx g @nx/nest:app apps/backend --unitTestRunner=none --e2eTestRunner=none
Step 4: Install Dependencies
# Backend GraphQL & Apollo Server
npm install @nestjs/graphql @nestjs/apollo @apollo/server graphql @as-integrations/express5# Database and Validation
npm install @nestjs/typeorm typeorm mssql class-validator class-transformer @nestjs/config
# Frontend Apollo Client & Material UI
npm install apollo-angular @apollo/client graphql graphql-tag @angular/material
# To configure Angular Material automatically, run:
npx nx g @angular/material:ng-add --project=frontend(use the "@angular/cdk": "^21.2.14" otherwise it will cause error while configuration of Angular Material )
Step 5: Generate the Employee Modules
Instead of creating files by hand, we use the CLI generators to spin up clean skeletons:
# Backend Employee Resource (GraphQL Code-First)
nx g @nx/nest:resource apps/backend/src/app/employee/employee --type=graphql-code-first# Frontend Feature Component & Service
npx nx g @nx/angular:component apps/frontend/src/app/pages/employee-list/employee-list --standalone --style=scss
npx nx g @nx/angular:service employee --project=frontend --path=apps/frontend/src/app/pages/services
Part 2: Understanding GraphQL Mechanics
Before writing backend code, we need to clarify the fundamental mechanics of GraphQL:
1. The Schema (The Contract)
In REST APIs, changes to the response can accidentally break the frontend. A GraphQL schema prevents this by defining exactly what data the API can send and receive, acting as a contract between the frontend and backend.
With the Code-First approach, you define the schema using TypeScript classes and decorators, and NestJS automatically generates the schema.gql file.
💡 Code-First: Write TypeScript classes, and NestJS creates the GraphQL schema for you automatically.
2. Queries vs. Mutations

• Queries (Read Operations): Used strictly to fetch data. Unlike REST where an endpoint returns every single database column (over-fetching), a GraphQL query allows the frontend to explicitly select only the fields it needs to display.

• Mutations (Write Operations): Used whenever we change data state (Create, Update, Delete). Mutations can optionally return the updated object, allowing the client to receive the latest data in the same request.
Part 3: Backend Implementation (NestJS + GraphQL + TypeORM)
1. Database Entity
This file acts as a dual-purpose blueprint. It sets the rules for both our SQL database table and our GraphQL type layout at the same time.
apps/backend/src/app/employee/entities/employee.entity.ts
import { Entity, PrimaryGeneratedColumn, Column } from 'typeorm';
import { ObjectType, Field, ID } from '@nestjs/graphql';@ObjectType() // Tells GraphQL this is an object it can return
@Entity() // Tells TypeORM to make a database table out of this
export class Employee {
@Field(() => ID)
@PrimaryGeneratedColumn('increment') // Auto-creates 1, 2, 3... IDs in the DB
id: number;
@Field()
@Column()
firstName: string;
@Field()
@Column()
lastName: string;
@Field()
@Column()
email: string;
@Field()
@Column()
department: string;
}
Understanding Decorators
If you’re new to NestJS or Angular, you’ll notice many functions starting with the @ symbol. These are called decorators. A decorator attaches metadata to a class, property, or method. Frameworks like NestJS, GraphQL, and TypeORM read this metadata at runtime to understand how your application should behave.

• @Entity() tells TypeORM that this class represents a database table. When the application starts, TypeORM maps this class to a table in SQL Server.

• @ObjectType() tells GraphQL that this class can be returned in queries and mutations. Without it, GraphQL won’t include this class in the generated schema.

• @Column() tells TypeORM that this property should become a column in the database table. Without it, the property exists only in the TypeScript class and won't be stored in SQL Server.

• @Field() tells GraphQL to expose this property in the API schema. Without it, clients cannot query or mutate this field.
2. Input DTOs
DTO stands for Data Transfer Object. A DTO defines the shape of data exchanged between the client and the server. Rather than accepting arbitrary JSON, DTOs explicitly define which fields are allowed and validate them before the request reaches our business logic.
We’ll create two DTOs:

• CreateEmployeeInput

• UpdateEmployeeInput
apps/backend/src/app/employee/dto/create-employee.input.ts
import { InputType, Field } from '@nestjs/graphql';
import { IsNotEmpty, IsEmail } from 'class-validator';@InputType() // Tells GraphQL: This data structure is coming from an incoming user submission form
export class CreateEmployeeInput {
@Field()
@IsNotEmpty({ message: 'First name cannot be empty' })
firstName: string;
@Field()
@IsNotEmpty({ message: 'Last name cannot be empty' })
